sessions.details: Recorded: 1980-05-14 (note that this is a British band quite separate from the later American band of the same name).
Recorded on 1977-11-21. First broadcast on John Peel's Show on 28th November 1977.

track_listing.track: Back In The Band (v/a 12" - Muthas Pride) EMI
Midnight Rambler (LP - Get Yer Ya-Ya's Out! - The Rolling Stones In Concert) Decca
Big Deal
Shot Down In The Night (7" - Live - Shot Down In The Night) Bronze
Feel Like Makin’ Love' (LP - Straight Shooter) Island
My Sex
'Danger Street' (LP - Rocks, Pebbles And Sand) Epic
Just Another Game
Ain’t No Love In The Heart Of The City (LP - Live At Hammersmith) Polydor
Break of Day (LP - No Fright) A&M
Artificial Life
Friday Night Connection
One Glass Of Whisky
There’s A Light (LP - Touch) Deram
The Man Who Dies Every Day
The Iceman (LP - Danger Zone) Capitol
Emerald (LP - Jailbreak) Vertigo
Star Cycle (LP - There And Back) Epic
Rock ’n’ Roll Runner
Paranoid (LP - Live At Last) Nems
Hide In Your Shell (LP - Crime Of The Century) A&M
School’s Out (LP - The Alice Cooper Show) Warner Bros.

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well hello there. Thomas Vance here. TV on the radio with a far from puerile radio program.
This is the Friday Rock Show and this evening we are more than happy to repeat a 1977 and indeed the only session ever recorded by the original Ultravox lineup.
Also we have the first ever session by a band from Luton called Toad the Wet Sprocket.
And we've got some new stuff from Hawkwind, Stanley Clark, Live Wire, Touch, Sammy Hagar, Jeff Beck and Face Dancer.
And some classic cuts by the Rolling Stones, Bad Company and Thin Lizzy, among others.
Not forgetting that time of the week when every brain cell counts, the Friday Night Connection.
And we start with a band called Quartz.
Live at the Madison Square Garden in November 1969, it was of course the Rolling Stones.
On the album Get Your Yaya's Out, the track was Midnight Rambler.
And I played that for Ken and Jackie and Steve and Diane, and also for Mike Harris, who lives at No. 37 Belgrave Road, Belgrave in Tamworth in Staffordshire.
And prior to that, you heard Quartz from a 12-incher, which is out on EMI.
The track that you heard from Quartz was called Back in the Band.
Also on the 12-incher, you'll find Wildfire, White Spirit, and Baby Jane.
It's 10.13, and now here's the first of four from Mick Mustafa, Martin Whitewick, Peter Austin, and Curly on the guitar,
better known as Toad the Wet Sprocket.
And that's an amazing new single by Hawkwind.
It's just come out on the bronze label
and it's called Shot Down in the Night.
Played that specially for a gentleman by the name of Peter
who lives in Stafford.
And I have to send lots of love to Peter for me.
Lady by the name of Sue,
when she wrote me this card upon which is printed
a photograph of the shambles in York,
she said she was feeling loony and depressed
with exams all in there.
Well, I would imagine by now Sue, you've got them all over
and I trust that you passed.
Also, the Hawkwind single was for Jill and Mandy and Debbie and Gina
and Sarah and Dino and John and Dennis and Sutton
and Bob the Absent-Minded and K.N.
and all the rockers at Cottingham High School in North Humberside
from Mike Cross.
1023, and before that you heard Toad the Wet Sprocket and Big Deal.
And here's a real classic.
There's a man who's endowed with more talent than most,
that of course was Stanley Clark,
and a track from his new album which is out on Epic.
The album's called Rocks, Pebbles and Sound,
and the track was Danger Street.
And just prior to that, you heard the first of about three we're playing tonight,
My Ultravox, recorded in 1977,
back in the days when John Fox was on the vocals.
The track was My Sex.
Prior to that, especially for Jackie and all the family and everybody else who knows,
P.J. Timores of Waverly, Wattling Street, Strood in Rochester and Kent,
I played a real classic from the album Straight Shooter,
Bad Company and Feel Like Making Love.
Friday Rock Show is 23 minutes now before 11.
Here's more from Sprocket Wet the Toad.
White Snake at Hammersmith.
That, of course, was White Snake.
Who? Snake.
And ain't no love in the heart of the city.
And next week on the program,
we will be repeating their set that was recorded at Reading last year.
Want to see them?
This week at the Hammersmith Odeon, Magic.
Play that for loads of people.
On behalf of Andy Carter,
that's Sue Mutterley and Melinda George and Paul Elsie and Tim and James
and Ian the Super Tonker, whatever that is,
and Sue Henry and Kieron, and Derek and Gong, Blow Wave, it says here,
and also for Alex and Graham Bristlehead and Joe the Hippo and John and Danny and Wombat the Evil,
and everybody from RTC in Southampton.
And also for Stephen Knowles, who lives over there in St. Phil Road in Belfast,
and for Dickie and Rob and Ray and Henry and Fossey and Leezy and all the band,
including Biff at No. 18, Bodmin Road in Tildesley.
Little communication here that has on the front of it a David Hamilton picture,
not the disc jockey who is now on radio too
but the photographer
the picture is bonnet and bouquet
and I'll read this out to you
notwithstanding that half of the upper fourth, three quarters of the lower fifth
and all of the upper fifth of Sidcott, School, Winscombe and Avon
regularly listen to your excellent programme
on the trannies, tuners and various other machines
under or over the bedclothes, pillows and duvets
and whereas a certain number of the staff
is contracted to tape anything worth listening to
which is most everything
and therefore can't be on patrol around the dorms
to confiscate any of your foreset equipment
We do therefore most earnestly enjoin you to give us a plug on your program
and to dedicate a record to Limey and the Crinoid
with lots and lots of love from all the leavers.
And I dedicate to you Toad the Wet Sprocket, which you heard in just another game.
And this here is Live Wire.
If you want to get out from under the wheel, you better get yourself a heart of steel.
Not a love, not a love, not a love made of waste.
They're wearing a utility drag.
The girls slip identikits from their utility bags.
Some refugees from suburbia are laughing, examining each other's gags.
It gets late, it begins to sound.
This is Billy Curry going bananas on the violin.
A track from 1977, an archive session of that year by Ultra Rocks.
The track was Artificial Life.
And before that, you heard something by Live Wire from their album called No Fright.
It's out on A&M.
The track was Break of Day.
Three minutes to 11 on the Friday Rock Show, and it's time for the Friday Night Connection.
The tracks we played you last week were these.
Saturday Night Special by Lynyrd Skynyrd from the LP One More for the Road.
Saturday Night's Alright for Fighting by Elton John from the album Goodbye, Yellow Brick Road.
and touch too much by acdc from the lp highway to hell a lot of people thought the answer was night
wrong the answer was one more for the road goodbye yellow brick road highway to hell
highways roads various things like that he's got the 10 pound record token well i'm not going to
tell you it is a gentleman by the name of michael lifko who lives near stoke-on-trent
Congratulations, Michael.
When you get it, spend it wisely.
There's a lot of good music about.
Part of the deal, as always, is to put together a Friday Night Connection of your own.
And Michael's done just that.
Three bits of music with a theme.
I would think that any self-respecting fan of this program, or indeed rock fan anywhere in the world,
should get that one, because it's easy.
Easy.
And that's the Friday Night Connection set tonight by Michael Lithgow, who lives in Stoke-on-Trent.
If you think you know what the answer is, jot it down on a postcard, in a letter, anywhere or any way you like.
And send it to the Friday Night Connection, the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, Broadcasting House, London W1A 4WW.
And if yours is the one that we drag out of the bag, well, you'll get your £10 record token.
But don't forget to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
Congratulations, Mr. Lithgow, 10-pound record token on its way to you.
And incidentally, thanks a million to everybody who put on their cards and letters last week,
whether they listened on the Medium Wave Band or on the VHF Band.
We thank you.
It's 11.12, and here's Toad the Wet Sprocket and one glass of whiskey.
That's an American band by the name of Touch, whom I'm told come from somewhere like Cincinnati.
Cincinnati, well, that place over there.
And the album's called Touch, and it's out on Areola, and that wasn't the track I was supposed to play at all.
You heard There's a Light.
I was supposed to play Don't You Know What Love Is,
but I figured that this time of night you probably did.
Now, before that, you heard Toad the Wet Sprocket
and a track from their first ever radio session done for us
called One Glass of Whiskey.
And you can see them tomorrow night in the hometown of Luton
at the King's Way Tavern.
And now, Ultravox.

and that track was Star Cycle.
20 to Midnight.

of 473 The Old Parker Road there in Belfast
to say hello to Tucker and Rossi and Donal and I.T. and Joe and Deidre and especially Elaine.
Also, hello to everybody who attends the Bankhead Academy,
that bastion of academics up there in Buckburn and Aberdeen.
Jill sends her best wishes, particularly to the boy that she stood next to
at the Thin Lizzy concert in Dundee on the 3rd of May,
whose name she doesn't know, but whose image she doesn't seem to forget.
And that Lizzy track was also for Janet Skerritt, who lives in Solihull,
The posh part of the West Midlands, I was told once.
I hope you're still up, Janet.
Rock and roll runner, Toad the Wet Sprocket.
Black Sabbath there with Ozzy, Ozzy, Ozzy on the microphone.

Paranoid.

But there you go.
It's available.

sounds like a Jim Davidson joke, but actually

That's 1956, part 2 of 25 Years of Rock, Sunday, 7 o'clock.
Hello there, this is Roger Daughtry,
and if you like Liquid Gold, Lena Martell, The Smurfs,
Max Bygraves, well, don't bother to tune in on Sunday at 3 o'clock,
because I ain't playing any of them.
To find out what I am playing, well, tune in at 3 o'clock.

I always love to listen to that programme because it gives you a great representation of what rock artists and various, if you like, all sorts of singers and people in the business like and play in their own houses.
And that way you sort of work out what their influences are.
And influences, I think, are one of the most interesting things about musicians.
The way you can sort of chart how they develop over the years by virtue of what they've heard, things like that.
I'm going to play you something now which is gentle and soft
and is super tramp.
...prime of the century, came out years ago,
but still sounds absolutely fresh,
and that was specially for Aaron Greenwood,
who lives in Belfast in Northern Ireland.
Lots of Belfast requests tonight, I'm happy to say.
And also for Chris and for Sue,
whom I'm sure are up to absolutely no good.
And here's Alice Cooper.
Alice Cooper, in Schools Out.

And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show.
I hope you enjoyed Ultra Rocks.
